name: Big-O: Runtime & Memory Complexity
description: Complexity analysis from scratch: reading the growth class off code, every class from O(1) to O(n!), space complexity, calculation rules, and Big-O vs Theta vs Omega. From the komplexitas tutorial.

topics.name: Big-O Basics & Why It Matters
topics.description: What Big-O measures, the two simplification rules, and common misconceptions.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Big-O notation describes how much slower a piece of code gets as its input grows. It does not measure seconds. It answers one question: if I give this code 10 times more data, does the work grow 10 times (O(n)), 100 times (O(n²)), or not at all (O(1))?

The same notation is used for two things: running time (how much the CPU works) and memory (how much extra space the code needs). Every algorithm has both, and they are independent.

## What problem does it solve?

Measuring speed in seconds is useless for comparing algorithms: it depends on the machine, the language, and the load. Big-O removes all of that and keeps only the shape of the growth. An algorithm that is fine with 10 items can be hopeless with 10 million — Big-O tells you this in advance.

## How it works

You simplify the exact operation count using two rules:

- Drop constant multipliers: 5n becomes O(n). Doing something 5 times per item or 1 time per item is the same growth shape.
- Keep only the biggest term: n² + 100n + 500 becomes O(n²), because when n is large, the n² part dwarfs everything else.

One warning: Big-O says nothing about small inputs. An O(n²) function can be faster than an O(n) one when n is small, because the O(n) one may have big fixed costs. Big-O is about what happens when n gets large.

## Common mistakes

- Quoting Big-O as if it measured seconds ("O(n) is fast") instead of growth
- Forgetting that an O(n²) solution can beat O(n) on small inputs
- Giving only time complexity when the interviewer also expects space

## Key terms

- O(1), O(n), O(n²) — growth classes: constant, linear, quadratic
- Worst case — Big-O usually describes the slowest possible run
- Dominant term — the part of the formula that grows fastest
- Time vs space complexity — CPU work vs extra memory, stated separately

topics.name: Complexity Classes: O(1) to O(n!)
topics.description: The growth classes with recognition patterns and canonical examples, including the two-pointer palindrome case study.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The complexity classes are the standard "speed categories" every algorithm falls into. From fastest to slowest: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!).

## What problem does it solve?

Instead of analyzing every algorithm from scratch, you learn to recognize a handful of patterns in code and instantly know how it scales.

## How it works

- O(1) — constant: the input size does not matter. Example: reading arr[3], or getting a value from a hash map. The position can be computed directly, so it is one step whether the array has 10 or 10 million items.
- O(log n) — logarithmic: each step throws away half of the remaining work. Example: binary search in a sorted array — check the middle, discard the wrong half, repeat. 1,000 items need ~10 steps, 1,000,000 need only ~20.
- O(n) — linear: you touch each item once. Example: summing an array, or checking if a string is a palindrome with two pointers (one from each end, comparing pairs — about n/2 comparisons, and only 2 index variables, so O(1) memory).
- O(n log n): the class of good sorting algorithms (merge sort, quicksort on average). Sorting splits the data log n times, and each level does n work.
- O(n²) — quadratic: a loop inside a loop, both over the input. Example: comparing every item with every other item to find duplicates. 1,000 items = 1,000,000 operations. Often fixable: use a Set to remember seen items — O(n) time for O(n) memory.
- O(2ⁿ) and O(n!) — exponential and factorial: the work doubles (or worse) with each extra item. Example: naive recursive Fibonacci, which recomputes the same values again and again, or generating all permutations. Unusable beyond tiny inputs — memoization or dynamic programming is the way out.

## Common mistakes

- Calling a nested loop O(n) because "it's just two loops" — it multiplies
- Claiming binary search works on unsorted data
- Missing that the Set-based duplicate fix trades O(n) memory for the speed

## Key terms

- Binary search — halving search in sorted data, O(log n)
- Two-pointer technique — one pointer from each end, O(n) time, O(1) space
- Nested loop — the telltale sign of O(n²)
- Memoization — caching results so exponential recursion becomes linear
- Space–time trade-off — spending memory (a Set, a cache) to save time

topics.name: Space Complexity
topics.description: Measuring extra memory: data structures, the call stack, and why time and space are independent.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Space complexity measures how much EXTRA memory an algorithm needs beyond its input, as the input grows. It uses the same O() notation as running time.

## What problem does it solve?

Two algorithms can take the same time but very different memory. Checking a palindrome with two pointers uses O(1) space (just two index variables); doing it by reversing the string uses O(n) space (a full copy). On big inputs, or on memory-limited machines, this difference decides which one you can actually run.

## How it works

There are two main sources of memory use:

- New data structures: if you build an array, map or set that grows with the input (a copy, a Set of all items), that is O(n) space. A few plain variables (a sum, two pointers) is O(1).
- The call stack: every recursive call adds a frame to the stack. A recursive factorial(n) stacks n frames before unwinding, so it uses O(n) memory even though it creates no visible data structure. Recurse too deep and the program crashes with a stack overflow.

The habit to build: always state both figures. "O(n) time and O(1) space" is a complete answer; "O(n)" alone is half of one.

## Common mistakes

- Ignoring the call stack — recursive solutions are not O(1) space
- Counting the input itself as extra space
- Saying "O(n)" without stating whether it is time or memory

## Key terms

- Auxiliary space — the extra memory, not counting the input itself
- Call stack — where function calls live; recursion depth = memory
- Stack overflow — crash when recursion goes too deep
- In-place algorithm — works in O(1) extra space by modifying the input

topics.name: Calculating Complexity & Asymptotic Notations
topics.description: Practical rules for deriving Big-O from code, plus Big-O vs Theta vs Omega and the little-o/little-omega strict bounds.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A set of practical rules for reading the Big-O of a function directly from its code, plus the related notations (Θ, Ω) you may be asked about.

## What problem does it solve?

In an interview you get a function and the question "what is its complexity?". These rules let you answer by looking at the structure of the code, without deep math.

## How it works

- Steps that run one after another ADD up, and the biggest wins: an O(n) loop followed by an O(n²) double loop is O(n²) overall.
- Nested loops MULTIPLY: a loop of n with a loop of n inside is O(n²); a loop of n with a binary search inside is O(n log n).
- Constants disappear: looping over half the array (n/2) is still O(n).
- Halving in a loop means log: any loop that cuts the remaining work in half each round runs O(log n) times.
- Different inputs stay separate: a function over two lists of sizes a and b is O(a + b) if the loops are sequential, O(a × b) if nested. Do not merge them into one n.

## The other notations

Big-O is an upper bound — "it is at most this bad" (worst case). Ω (Omega) is the lower bound — the best case. Θ (Theta) means the bound is exact — best and worst are the same shape. In practice everyone uses Big-O, because the worst case is the useful guarantee. The average case matters when the worst case is rare and misleading — quicksort is O(n log n) on average but O(n²) in its rare worst case.

There are also strict ("little") versions: little-o means "grows strictly slower" (n = o(n²) is true, n = o(n) is not). Think of O as ≤ and o as <. These are theory; interviews almost always want big-O.

## Common mistakes

- Merging two different input sizes into one n (it is O(a + b), not O(n²))
- Adding nested loops instead of multiplying them
- Quoting the best case when the interviewer asked for the usual (worst-case) bound

## Key terms

- Sequential steps add, nested loops multiply — the two core rules
- Worst / average / best case — O / (context) / Ω
- Θ (Theta) — exact growth rate
- O(a + b) — keep separate inputs separate

questions.title: What is Big-O?
questions.prompt: Explain what Big-O notation actually measures and the rules you use to simplify an expression like 3n² + 50n + 200.
questions.answerStructureHint: Define what it measures (growth, not seconds) → the two simplification rules → what it does NOT mean.

expectedPoints.label: Growth rate, not wall-clock time
expectedPoints.description: Measures how work grows with input size n, not seconds — machine and language independent.

expectedPoints.label: Drops constant factors
expectedPoints.description: 5n is O(n); constants do not change the shape of growth.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps only the dominant term
expectedPoints.description: n² + 100n + 500 is O(n²) because n² dominates for large n.

expectedPoints.label: Applies to time AND space
expectedPoints.description: The same notation describes CPU time and memory; an algorithm has both.

followUps.prompt: Can an O(n²) implementation ever be the right choice over an O(n) one?

expectedPoints.label: Small n / constant overhead nuance
expectedPoints.description: For small inputs, constants dominate; O(n²) with tiny constants can win.

questions.title: Why is binary search O(log n)?
questions.prompt: Why is binary search O(log n), and what precondition does it require?

expectedPoints.label: Each step halves the search space
expectedPoints.description: Inspect the middle, discard the half that cannot contain the target.

expectedPoints.label: log₂(n) steps to one element
expectedPoints.description: 1000 elements → ~10 steps, a million → ~20.

expectedPoints.label: Requires sorted input
expectedPoints.description: The halving argument only works on a sorted array.

followUps.prompt: Beyond binary search, how do you recognize O(log n) behaviour in code or data structures?

expectedPoints.label: Recognition pattern
expectedPoints.description: Halving/thirding per step instead of one-by-one; balanced trees (BST) search/insert.

questions.title: Palindrome check: time and space
questions.prompt: Walk me through checking whether a string is a palindrome, and give the time and space complexity of your approach versus the naive reverse-and-compare solution.
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe two pointers → state O(n) time / O(1) space → contrast with reverse-and-compare's O(n) space.

expectedPoints.label: Two-pointer technique
expectedPoints.description: One pointer from the front, one from the back, comparing pairs toward the middle.

expectedPoints.label: O(n) time
expectedPoints.description: About n/2 comparisons; every character touched at most once.

expectedPoints.label: O(1) space vs naive O(n)
expectedPoints.description: Two indices only; reversing creates an n-sized copy.

questions.title: Speeding up an O(n²) duplicate search
questions.prompt: You find a duplicate-detection function with a nested loop comparing every pair. How would you make it faster, and what is the trade-off?

expectedPoints.label: Hash set in one pass
expectedPoints.description: Walk once, add each element to a Set; if already present, it is a duplicate → O(n) time.

expectedPoints.label: Space–time trade-off named
expectedPoints.description: O(n) time is bought with O(n) memory — the classic trade.

expectedPoints.label: Explains why nested loops are O(n²)
expectedPoints.description: Every element compared with every other: n × n operations; n=1000 → a million.

questions.title: Exponential blow-up and how to escape it
questions.prompt: The naive recursive Fibonacci is O(2ⁿ). Explain why, and how you would bring it down.

expectedPoints.label: Two branches per call, recomputing subresults
expectedPoints.description: Each call spawns two more; the same fib(k) values are recomputed over and over.

expectedPoints.label: Memoization / DP fix
expectedPoints.description: Caching subresults makes it O(n); dynamic programming or iteration also works.

expectedPoints.label: Sense of scale
expectedPoints.description: 2ⁿ at n=50 is ~10¹⁵ operations; factorial is even worse (20! ≈ 2.4×10¹⁸).

questions.title: Space complexity and the call stack
questions.prompt: What does space complexity measure, and why does a recursive factorial use O(n) memory even though it allocates no data structures?

expectedPoints.label: Extra memory beyond the input
expectedPoints.description: How auxiliary memory grows with n; main sources are new structures and the call stack.

expectedPoints.label: Recursion depth = stack frames
expectedPoints.description: Every recursive call pushes a frame; factorial(n) stacks n frames → O(n) space, risk of stack overflow.

expectedPoints.label: Time and space are independent
expectedPoints.description: Two O(n)-time algorithms can differ in space (palindrome two-pointer O(1) vs reverse O(n)); state both.

questions.title: Deriving complexity from code
questions.prompt: Give me your practical rules for deriving the Big-O of a function: sequential steps, nested loops, and functions taking two inputs of different sizes.

expectedPoints.label: Sequential steps add, dominant wins
expectedPoints.description: O(n) + O(n²) = O(n²).

expectedPoints.label: Nested loops multiply
expectedPoints.description: Outer n × inner n = O(n²); outer n × inner log n = O(n log n).

expectedPoints.label: Separate inputs stay separate
expectedPoints.description: Two sizes a and b give O(a + b) or O(a × b) — not collapsed into O(n²).

followUps.prompt: What is the difference between Big-O, Theta, and Omega — and when does the average case matter?

expectedPoints.label: O/Θ/Ω distinction
expectedPoints.description: Upper bound (worst) / tight bound / lower bound (best); quicksort as the average-case example.
